phase_metadata.title: CSS Layout Basics
phase_metadata.description:
Mastering the positioning and display behavior of elements,
understanding the z-axis, and using flexible CSS units.

learning_objectives:
Differentiate between block, inline, and
inline-block display behaviors

Master the five types of CSS positioning:
static, relative, absolute, fixed, and sticky

Control the stacking order of
elements using z-index

Understand legacy layout methods like float and
clear

Choose appropriate CSS units (px, em, rem, %, vh, vw) for responsive
design
introduction:
By default, the
browser stacks elements one after another in what we call the 'Normal Document
Flow'. However, modern websites require elements to overlap, stay fixed while
scrolling, or sit side-by-side. This phase teaches you the fundamental tools to
break the default flow and place elements exactly where they belong.

concept_explanation.display_property:
The 'display' property is the most
important tool for controlling layout. 'block' elements take the full width;
'inline' elements only take enough width for their content; 'inline-block'
behaves like an inline element but allows you to set width and height.
concept_explanation.positioning:
Positioning allows you to move an element from its normal spot.
'Relative' moves it relative to its original position. 'Absolute' moves it
relative to its nearest positioned parent. 'Fixed' sticks it to the screen, and
'Sticky' toggles between relative and fixed based on scroll.
concept_explanation.z_index:
When
elements overlap, z-index determines which one stays on top. A higher z-index
appears 'closer' to the user. It only works on positioned elements.
concept_explanation.css_units:
Units are categorized into 'Absolute' (px) which stay the same
size, and 'Relative' (em, rem, %, vh, vw) which scale based on parent size, root
font size, or the browser window (viewport).

breakdown_of_syntax.display_none:
Completely removes the element from
the page; it takes up no space.
breakdown_of_syntax.display_block:
Starts on a new line and
takes up the full width available.
breakdown_of_syntax.top_right_bottom_left:
Offsets used with
positioned elements to move them.
breakdown_of_syntax.relative_position:
Moves an element
without affecting the space it originally occupied.
breakdown_of_syntax.absolute_position:
Removes the element from the document flow; other elements act as if it's not
there.
breakdown_of_syntax.viewport_units:
1vh is 1% of the window height; 1vw is 1% of the
window width.

rules_and_key_points.rule_1:
Inline elements ignore
width and height properties.
rules_and_key_points.rule_2:
Block elements always start on a new
line.
rules_and_key_points.rule_3:
Position: absolute only works relative to a parent that has a
position other than 'static'.
rules_and_key_points.rule_4:
Z-index requires the element to have
a position (relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky).
rules_and_key_points.rule_5:
Fixed elements
are positioned relative to the browser window (viewport).
rules_and_key_points.rule_6:
Relative
positioning keeps the 'ghost' of the element in the document flow (space is
reserved).
rules_and_key_points.rule_7:
Use 'rem' for font sizes to ensure accessibility
(scaling with browser settings).
rules_and_key_points.rule_8:
Use '%' for widths inside
containers to make them fluid.
rules_and_key_points.rule_9:
Floating an element removes it from
the normal flow but allows text to wrap around it.
rules_and_key_points.rule_10:
Clear: both is
used to stop elements from wrapping around floated items.

code_example_1.title: Display and Units Comparison
code_example_1.description:
Visualizing block vs
inline-block and relative units.

real_world_use_cases:
Creating a 'Fixed' navigation bar that stays at the
top while scrolling.
Placing a 'Sale' icon over a product image.

Building
a modal/popup that covers the entire screen (position: fixed).

Centering
elements perfectly using relative units.

Creating a 'Sticky' table header
that stays visible while reading long rows.
common_mistakes:
Using
position: absolute without setting the parent to position: relative.
Expecting z-index to work on static elements.

Using px for everything,
making the website look tiny on mobile screens.

Forgetting that fixed
elements overlap other content (you need padding on the body).

Using display:
none to hide things you want screen readers to see (use visibility: hidden or
other techniques instead).
best_practices:
Use 'rem' for typography and
'em' for padding/margins inside components.

Use 'position: relative' as a
safe anchor for absolute children.

Keep z-index values low and organized
(don't use 999999).

Avoid using 'float' for layouts; use Flexbox or Grid
(coming in future phases).
Use 'vh' and 'vw' for full-screen hero sections.

faqs.question: What is the difference between em and rem?
faqs.answer:
rem is relative to the root (html) font-size, while em is relative to
the font-size of its immediate parent.

faqs.question:
Why is my element
disappearing when I set position: absolute?
faqs.answer:
It might be behind
another element (needs z-index) or its parent has overflow: hidden and you moved
the element outside the parent's boundaries.

summary:
Phase 8 covered
the mechanics of layout. You learned how to change element behavior with
display, how to move them precisely with position, how to layer them with
z-index, and how to use modern relative units to build flexible designs.
key_takeaways:
Block elements take 100% width; Inline elements take content
width.
Inline-block allows width/height on inline elements.

Position:
relative moves an element without losing its space.

Position: absolute
requires a positioned parent.

Position: fixed is relative to the screen, not
the page.
Z-index only works on positioned elements.
vh and vw are based on the viewport size.

Floats
are for wrapping text around images.

Position: sticky is a hybrid of relative
and fixed.
preview_next_topic:
In Phase 9, we will learn Flexbox—the
modern way to align elements and create responsive layouts with ease.

questions_and_answers.question:
What is the default 'position' value
for all HTML elements?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The default value is 'static'. Static
elements follow the normal document flow and ignore top, right, bottom, left,
and z-index properties.

questions_and_answers.question:
Explain
how 'rem' units help with website accessibility.
questions_and_answers.answer:
rem units are
based on the user's browser settings. If a visually impaired user increases
their default font size, a website using rem will scale up proportionally,
whereas a site using px will remain fixed and potentially unreadable.

questions_and_answers.question:
Write the CSS to make a  take
up exactly half the width and half the height of the browser window.

How do you center a fixed navigation bar that has a width
of 500px?

Identify the bug: A student applies z-index: 10 to a paragraph
to make it appear over an image, but it stays behind the image. Why?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The paragraph likely has its default position: static. Z-index only works on
elements that have a position value of relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky.

questions_and_answers.question:
Why does a container with
'height: 100%' often fail to fill the screen?
questions_and_answers.answer:
Percentage heights
are relative to the parent. If the parent (like  or ) doesn't have a defined
height, 100% of 'nothing' results in 0 height. Use '100vh' for a guaranteed
full-screen height.

questions_and_answers.question:
Create a
CSS rule for a 'sidebar' that stays fixed on the left side of the screen,
is 200px wide, and occupies the full height.

Write code for a parent 'box' (300px)
containing a child 'circle'. The circle should be positioned exactly in the
bottom-right corner, overlapping the parent's border by 20px.

multiple_choice_questions.question:
Which display value
allows an element to have a width and height but stay on the same line as other
elements?

An element with
'position: absolute' is positioned relative to:
If the root font-size is 16px, how many pixels is 2.5rem?

Predict the behavior: An
element has 'position: fixed; bottom: 0;'.

options.A:
It stays at the
bottom of the current section
options.B:
It stays at the bottom of the browser
window even when scrolling
options.D:
It moves to the bottom
of the HTML document

multiple_choice_questions.question:
What happens to the space occupied by an element when you set
'display: none'?

options.A: The space is reserved but invisible
options.B: The space is removed from the layout

multiple_choice_questions.question: Find the bug: div { position: static; z-index: 50; }

options.A: Static elements cannot have a z-index

multiple_choice_questions.question:
Find the bug: section { width: 100vw;
height: 100vh; overflow: hidden; }

options.A:
vw and vh cannot be used
together
options.B: There is no bug, but it might hide content on small screens
options.D: Section is not a block element

multiple_choice_questions.question:
Complete the code to
make an element move relative to itself: .box { position: ____; top: 10px; }

Complete the code
to prevent text from wrapping around a float: .footer { ____: both; }

Which unit is best
for a container that should always be as wide as the screen?
